summary: The nasal septum is formed by septal cartilage, perpendicular plate of the ethmoid, and the vomer.
The nasal septum is formed by the frontal bone, maxilla, and palatine bone.
The nasal septum is formed entirely by the septal cartilage.
Little's area (Kiesselbach's plexus) on the anteroinferior septum is the site of 90% of epistaxis cases.
Little's area is located on the lateral wall of the nose and accounts for 50% of epistaxis.
Little's area is located in the nasopharynx and accounts for posterior epistaxis.
The ostiomeatal complex drains the maxillary, frontal, and anterior ethmoidal sinuses into the middle meatus.
The ostiomeatal complex drains all paranasal sinuses into the inferior meatus.
The ostiomeatal complex is located in the superior meatus.
The nasolacrimal duct drains into the inferior meatus of the nasal cavity.
The nasolacrimal duct drains into the middle meatus of the nasal cavity.
The nasolacrimal duct drains into the superior meatus of the nasal cavity.
The sphenopalatine artery is the largest artery supplying the nasal cavity and is a branch of the maxillary artery.
The anterior ethmoidal artery is the largest artery supplying the nasal cavity.
The sphenopalatine artery is a branch of the facial artery.
The posterior ethmoidal sinuses drain into the superior meatus, not the middle meatus.
The posterior ethmoidal sinuses drain into the middle meatus along with the anterior ethmoidal sinuses.
The posterior ethmoidal sinuses drain into the inferior meatus.
The sphenoidal sinus drains into the sphenoethmoidal recess above the superior turbinate.
The sphenoidal sinus drains into the middle meatus via the ostiomeatal complex.
The sphenoidal sinus drains directly into the nasopharynx.
